checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains only information directly present in the source PDF. All titles, headings, and body text (paragraphs and list items) have a clear basis in the original document. No fabricated content was detected.
The source document does not contain specific contact information like phone numbers or email addresses. The output JSON also contains no such information. No fabricated contact details were found.

All three pages of the source document are accounted for in the output JSON. Section 1 corresponds to page 1, section 2 to page 2, and section 3 to page 3. All textual components from these pages have been captured.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no tables are present in the output JSON to evaluate for missing rows.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: No significant text content is missing. Page 1's three main text blocks are present. Page 2's title and map content (described in alt text) are present. Page 3's title and all eight bullet points are faithfully reproduced in the JSON list element.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON matches the source document exactly, preserving specific terminology (e.g., 'US Survey Foot', 'International Foot', '1/11-1/12', 'Alexander') and formatting (superscript in '4th'). No wording changes that alter meaning were found.

checks.a_analysis: The JSON structure faithfully represents the text content from the source without duplication. Major text blocks like headings and paragraphs appear only once in the elements array corresponding to their source locations. While section titles in the JSON metadata often match heading text, this is a standard structural organization for navigation and does not constitute content duplication within the document flow.

The heading levels in the JSON are consistent (Level 2 for major page/section titles), which is an acceptable normalization for a multi-page presentation document where each page represents a major section. No levels are skipped (e.g., H1 to H3).
There are no tables in the source document or the converted JSON output.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical flow of the source document: it proceeds page-by-page, capturing headers, images, and then supporting text or lists. This matches the intended communication flow of the presentation slides.
The bulleted list on page 3 is correctly identified and structured as an unordered list in the JSON. The number of items (8) and the text content match the source perfectly.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The semantic.json contains 2 explicit heading elements and 3 section titles. The HTML metadata indicates 5 headings in the rendered HTML. This mismatch of 3 headings is exactly equal to the number of sections defined in the JSON. In accessible document conversion, renderers typically promote section titles to semantic headings (H1 or H2) to provide a navigation structure for screen readers. This is an expected behavior of the renderer to enhance accessibility and does not indicate a structural failure in the conversion pipeline.

There are no links in the source document or the semantic.json. The single link reported in the HTML metadata is likely a pipeline-added utility link (such as a skip link or a footer link for accessibility) which is an expected change.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document follows the logical vertical flow of the source presentation. Each page's main components (title, content, graphics) are presented in the correct sequence. While images are broken or missing (addressed in V3), the structural organization of information remains consistent with the source document's narrative flow.
There are no tables present in the source PDF document.

Significant meaningful visual content is missing or broken. The main map of North Carolina and the LiDAR system diagram from page 1 of the PDF are missing entirely from the HTML. The maps on pages 2 and 3 of the PDF, which show high-resolution topographic refresh areas, are rendered in the HTML as solid green squares. Additionally, the DPS branding logo in the header and the EMAP accreditation logo in the footer are absent.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 shows a map 'North Carolina LiDAR Quality Level By Phase' and a LiDAR scanning diagram. Pages 2 and 3 show detailed maps of Western NC counties.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map from Page 1 is missing below its header. The maps from Pages 2 and 3 are rendered as solid green squares. Logos for DPS and EMAP are missing.
checks.b_evidence_location: HTML output corresponding to PDF pages 1, 2, and 3.

checks.a_analysis: There is visual duplication of header text. The main title of the document is repeated twice at the very top of the HTML. Furthermore, the section heading '2025 Data Collection Area North Carolina Collaboratory' is repeated twice consecutively before the final bulleted list.
checks.b_evidence_source: PDF Page 1 has one title: 'NC Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) Elevation Data 2024 Data Collection'. PDF Page 3 has one title: '2025 Data Collection Area North Carolina Collaboratory'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The title 'NC Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) Elevation Data 2024 Data Collection' is repeated twice. The section title '2025 Data Collection Area North Carolina Collaboratory' is repeated twice.
checks.b_evidence_location: Top of the document and middle of the document.

checks.a_analysis: The overall visual quality is significantly degraded due to the presence of large, uninformative solid green blocks where detailed maps should be, and the unprofessional appearance of duplicated headers. This detracts from the document's utility and professional standing.
checks.b_evidence_source: Source PDF contains professional maps and layout.
checks.b_evidence_output: The output contains broken green image placeholders and repeated text blocks.
